narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your undergrad and LSAT prep—it's tough to hear that your law school application didn't make the cut based on our prediction model for bar exam success. Picture the model as an experienced advisor who's seen thousands of students like you navigate this path, and it looked at your profile to gauge your odds of passing the bar on the first try. What stood out most was your Year 3 decile of 2.0, which was the biggest signal pulling toward a higher risk of failure, suggesting that even later in law school, your grades landed in the lower ranks compared to peers. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 2.0, reinforcing a pattern of being in the second decile from the bottom early on, which made the model worry about building momentum. Your LSAT score of 34.5 also tipped the scales toward caution, as it didn't quite hit the stronger marks that often predict bar success in competitive programs. Layered on top was your middle family income bracket, which the model saw as less of a buffer compared to higher supports that help some students push through tough stretches. Your white racial background and female gender each added a subtle nudge toward the riskier side in the patterns the model has learned, while your solid 3.6 undergrad GPA and full-time study commitment helped a bit but couldn't fully counterbalance the rest. Together, these painted a picture of someone with potential but facing an uphill climb, landing at a 41% predicted chance of bar failure—enough to pause admission for now. This isn't a judgment on your worth or drive; it's just the model's way of spotting patterns to protect your time and dreams from a potential setback. Many students in similar spots have pivoted—maybe targeting schools with stronger support or boosting those deciles through targeted prep—and gone on to thrive. You've got a strong UGPA foundation; lean into that resilience, and consider reapplying after addressing those early grade patterns. We're rooting for you to rewrite this story.
